name: Yomi
description: Yomi, also known as Yomi-no-kuni (黄泉の国), is the land of the dead in Japanese mythology and Shinto cosmology. It is a perpetually dark and filthy realm, often depicted as being located beneath the earth, accessed through a dark and winding passage or cave. Unlike some other underworlds that offer judgment or reward, Yomi is primarily a place of stagnation and decay. Once one consumes food from Yomi, they are forever bound to it, making escape exceedingly difficult, if not impossible. The atmosphere of Yomi is oppressive, characterized by a sense of inescapable gloom and the ever-present threat of the decaying spirits that reside within. It is a place of defilement, contrasting sharply with the purity sought in Shinto rituals. The landscape itself is often described as barren and desolate, reflecting the spiritual emptiness of the dead who dwell there. Yomi serves as a stark reminder of the inevitability of death and the dangers of contact with the impure.
subtitle: The land of the dead in Japanese mythology.
location: Beneath the earth, accessed through a cave or passage.
significance: Represents the inevitable fate of death and the dangers of impurity.

keyMyths.title: Izanagi and Izanami's Descent
keyMyths.description: Izanagi, grieving the death of his wife Izanami during the birth of the fire god Kagutsuchi, descends to Yomi to retrieve her. He finds her transformed into a decaying corpse. Despite her warnings, he looks upon her, horrified by her state. Enraged, Izanami orders shikome (foul women) and yakusa-no-ikazuchi (thunder deities) to pursue him. He barely escapes, sealing the entrance to Yomi with a large boulder.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the Seasons
keyMyths.description: While not directly about Yomi itself, the separation of Izanagi and Izanami and the sealing of the entrance to Yomi is often interpreted as the mythological origin of death and the separation of the world of the living from the world of the dead. This separation contributes to the cyclical nature of life and death, and, by extension, the seasons.

keyMyths.title: The Pursuit by the Shikome
keyMyths.description: During Izanagi's escape from Yomi, Izanami sends shikome, hideous women, to chase after him. Izanagi throws down his headdress, which transforms into grapes, and his comb, which transforms into bamboo shoots, to distract them and slow their pursuit. This highlights the desperate measures one must take to escape the clutches of Yomi and its inhabitants.

sources.author: Chamberlain, Basil Hall
sources.description: An English translation of the Kojiki, one of the earliest written records of Japanese mythology and history. Provides a detailed account of the myth of Izanagi and Izanami's descent to Yomi.
sources.text: An English translation of the Kojiki, one of the earliest written records of Japanese mythology and history. Provides a detailed account of the myth of Izanagi and Izanami's descent to Yomi.

sources.author: Aston, W.G.
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important source for Japanese mythology. Offers a different perspective on the events surrounding Izanagi and Izanami.
sources.text: An English translation of the Nihon Shoki, another important source for Japanese mythology. Offers a different perspective on the events surrounding Izanagi and Izanami.

sources.author: Philippi, Donald L.
sources.description: A modern translation and annotation of the Kojiki, offering insights into the cultural and historical context of the myths.
sources.text: A modern translation and annotation of the Kojiki, offering insights into the cultural and historical context of the myths.

worshipPractices.name: Purification Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Because Yomi is considered a place of defilement, purification rituals (misogi) are performed to cleanse oneself after contact with death or related objects. These rituals often involve water and are meant to remove the taint associated with the land of the dead and restore spiritual purity.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings to Izanami
worshipPractices.description: While Yomi is not directly worshipped, offerings may be made to Izanami, the goddess residing there, particularly by those seeking solace after the loss of a loved one or to appease her spirit. These offerings are typically left at gravesites or ancestral shrines.

festivals.name: Obon Festival
festivals.description: During Obon, the spirits of ancestors are believed to return to the world of the living. While not solely focused on Yomi, the festival acknowledges the realm of the dead and honors deceased family members through offerings, dances, and the lighting of lanterns to guide the spirits back home.

festivals.name: Higan
festivals.timing: During the Spring and Autumn Equinoxes
festivals.description: Higan is a Buddhist holiday observed to commemorate deceased ancestors. Families visit gravesites to clean them, offer prayers, and leave food offerings, reflecting a connection with the realm where spirits reside, including the potential journey to or from Yomi.

cultural.modernLegacy: Yomi's influence is visible in modern Japanese media, particularly in anime, manga, and video games, where it often serves as a dark and dangerous setting for supernatural adventures. The concept of Yomi as a defiled underworld also contributes to the ongoing emphasis on cleanliness and purification rituals in Japanese culture and religious practices.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are deep, dark, and unpleasant realms located beneath the world, serving as a place of punishment and imprisonment, though Tartarus is specifically for the wicked and Titans.
Helheim is a dark and dismal realm ruled by Hel, similar to Yomi as a destination for the dead, though Helheim is specifically for those who die of sickness or old age.
The Duat is an underworld realm that the deceased must navigate, filled with challenges and dangers, mirroring Yomi's perilous nature and journey for the souls of the dead.

associations.name: Yellow
associations.significance: Yellow is associated with decay and corruption, reflecting Yomi's unclean and stagnant nature.

associations.name: Earth
associations.significance: Yomi is located beneath the earth, solidifying the connection between the underworld and the ground.

associations.name: Decay
associations.significance: Decay represents the inevitable transformation and corruption that occurs within Yomi.

associations.name: Maggot
associations.significance: Maggots are associated with corpses and decay, reflecting the state of the dead in Yomi.

associations.name: Mushroom
associations.significance: Mushrooms thrive in dark, damp environments, similar to the conditions of Yomi.

associations.name: Stagnation
associations.significance: Yomi represents a place of unchanging and unmoving existence after death.

associations.name: New Moon
associations.significance: The new moon symbolizes darkness and the absence of light, mirroring the perpetual gloom of Yomi.

companions.name: Izanami
companions.description: Izanami is Yomi's wife and the creator goddess who retreated to the underworld after death. Her presence profoundly shapes Yomi's nature, transforming it from a neutral realm into a place of decay and resentment.

companions.name: Shikome
companions.description: The Shikome are hideous female spirits that serve Izanami in Yomi. They are dispatched to chase and torment those who dare to trespass or anger the queen of the underworld.

companions.name: Yomotsu-shikome
companions.description: Similar to Shikome, these are terrifying women of the underworld, often depicted with disheveled hair and sharp claws. They are specifically associated with the filth and decay of Yomi.

companions.name: Yomotsu-heguri
companions.description: These are the Thunder Gods of Yomi, serving as protectors of the realm and enforcers of Izanami's will. They guard the entrance and prevent the living from easily escaping.

companions.name: Izanagi
companions.description: Izanagi, although initially a visitor, represents the boundary between the living and the dead. He becomes a protector of the living by sealing the entrance to Yomi, preventing the dead from escaping.

extendedContent.title: Geography and Features of Yomi-no-Kuni
extendedContent.content:
Yomi-no-Kuni, the Land of the Dead, is consistently portrayed as a subterranean realm, a dark and desolate mirror of the vibrant world above. Its entrance is often described as a shadowy passage or cave, a physical threshold between the living and the dead. While specific geographical features within Yomi are less detailed than in, for example, Greek or Norse underworlds, the overarching impression is one of decay and stagnation. Rivers of filth and perpetually dim lighting reinforce the sense of hopelessness and the irreversible transition from life to death. This stands in stark contrast to the perceived purity and harmony associated with the world of the living, emphasizing the polluting nature of death in Shinto beliefs.

Unlike idealized afterlives that promise paradise or reward, Yomi primarily represents the finality of death and the inevitable corruption of the physical body. The food of Yomi is a particularly significant element. Once consumed, it binds the individual to the underworld, making escape virtually impossible. This 'food of the dead' motif is found in various cultures and serves as a powerful symbol of the irreversible change that death brings. The landscape itself is often described through its lack of life, the absence of light, and the pervasive presence of miasma, further contributing to the understanding of Yomi as a place to be avoided rather than sought.

While not explicitly detailed on maps or described with precise coordinates, the symbolic geography of Yomi is crucial to understanding its significance in Japanese mythology. It serves as a potent reminder of mortality and the importance of maintaining purity and connection with the world of the living. The lack of specific landmarks within Yomi, beyond the entrance and the general sense of decay, reinforces the idea that death is a formless, indistinct void, a place where individual identity begins to dissolve.

extendedContent.title: Sacred Significance and Shinto Beliefs
extendedContent.content:
Yomi holds a complex and somewhat ambivalent position within Shinto beliefs. On one hand, it is a place of defilement and pollution (kegare), directly associated with death and decay, elements considered highly undesirable in Shinto practices. Contact with Yomi, or anything associated with death, requires purification rituals to restore balance and harmony. This aversion is reflected in Shinto shrines, which are typically located in areas of natural beauty and tranquility, far removed from graveyards or other places associated with death.

However, Yomi is not necessarily viewed as a realm of punishment. It is simply the destination for all souls, regardless of their actions in life. This contrasts with many other underworld mythologies where judgment and moral reckoning play a central role. The focus in Shinto is less on individual moral evaluation and more on the maintenance of ritual purity and the harmony of the cosmos. The Izanagi and Izanami myth demonstrates this concept clearly. Izanagi's journey to retrieve Izanami from Yomi and his subsequent purification ritual highlight the contaminating power of the underworld and the necessity of cleansing to restore balance to the world.

The connection between Yomi and the cycle of life and death is also significant. While Yomi represents the end of physical existence, Shinto beliefs emphasize the continuity of the spirit and the importance of ancestor veneration. The spirits of the deceased are believed to remain connected to the world of the living and can influence events for good or ill. Therefore, while Yomi itself is a place of defilement, the ancestors who reside there are revered and honored through rituals and offerings. This duality underscores the complex relationship between life and death in Shinto cosmology.

extendedContent.title: The Story of Izanagi and Izanami in Yomi
extendedContent.content:
The most prominent story associated with Yomi is undoubtedly the myth of Izanagi and Izanami, the divine couple who created the islands of Japan. After giving birth to numerous deities, Izanami dies while birthing the fire god Kagutsuchi. Grief-stricken, Izanagi journeys to Yomi to retrieve his beloved wife. He finds her in a decaying state, her body festering with maggots and demons. Despite Izanami's warning not to look upon her, Izanagi breaks his promise and lights a comb, revealing her horrifying form.

Appalled and terrified, Izanagi flees from Yomi, pursued by Izanami and the shikome (foul women) of the underworld. He uses various obstacles to slow her down, including throwing his headdress, which transforms into grapes, and his comb, which transforms into bamboo shoots. Finally, he blocks the entrance to Yomi with a massive boulder, severing the connection between the land of the living and the land of the dead. This act marks a crucial separation in Japanese mythology, establishing a clear boundary between life and death.

Enraged by Izanagi's betrayal, Izanami vows to kill one thousand people every day. Izanagi, in turn, vows to create one thousand five hundred new people each day, thus balancing the forces of life and death. This myth is foundational to understanding the Shinto perspective on death, pollution, and the necessary separation between the worlds of the living and the dead. It also highlights the power of ritual purification and the importance of maintaining boundaries to prevent the intrusion of defilement into the world of the living.

extendedContent.title: Yomi and Modern Connections
extendedContent.content:
While rooted in ancient mythology, the concept of Yomi continues to resonate in modern Japanese culture, albeit often in subtle or adapted forms. The fear of death and the associated pollution (kegare) still influence certain social customs and rituals. For example, practices surrounding funerals and mourning are carefully observed to ensure the proper separation between the deceased and the living, and to prevent the spread of defilement.

The imagery of Yomi, particularly its darkness and decay, has also found its way into popular culture, influencing depictions of the underworld in anime, manga, and video games. These portrayals often incorporate elements of traditional Japanese folklore and Shinto beliefs, while also adding modern interpretations and creative embellishments. For instance, some video games feature characters who must navigate treacherous landscapes and battle demonic creatures within a Yomi-inspired underworld.

Furthermore, the themes of mortality, grief, and the acceptance of death, which are central to the myth of Izanagi and Izanami, continue to be explored in contemporary Japanese literature and film. These works often grapple with the complexities of human existence and the search for meaning in the face of inevitable loss. By drawing upon the rich symbolism of Yomi, artists and writers can evoke powerful emotions and explore profound philosophical questions about the nature of life and death.
